ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for battered deep-fried chicken as a composite dish; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared food dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of 3 pieces of deep-fried battered chicken weighs approximately 270 g, representing a common hawker or fast-food portion in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Chicken — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for fried chicken (3 pc), CR ID F11020030. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken with mixed protein-fat-carbohydrate matrix
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific HPB fried chicken (3 pc) entry. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous battered fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 40–50 for battered/breaded chicken products due to the protein-fat matrix substantially blunting the glycaemic response of the wheat-flour batter. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 17.3) / 100 ≈ 7.8, using 270 g serving size and 6.42 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 553 mg per 100 g; saturated fat from deep-frying promotes arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid fried chicken due to its high sodium content (553 mg/100 g), which exceeds the 400 mg/100 g caution threshold, and should avoid additional dipping sauces that further increase sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-fried batter coating and high fat content impair insulin sensitivity; negligible dietary fibre (0.66 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid regular consumption of deep-fried battered chicken, as the high fat content and refined-carbohydrate batter impair insulin sensitivity and the negligible fibre (0.66 g/100 g) provides no meaningful glycaemic buffering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g), trans fat (0.2 g/100 g from frying oil), and high sodium (553 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit deep-fried chicken due to its saturated fat, trans fat from frying oil, and high sodium content, all of which contribute to dyslipidaemia, arterial stiffness, and elevated blood pressure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; trans fat and high sodium are pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried chicken as the AGEs generated during frying, combined with trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) and high sodium (553 mg/100 g), directly promote joint inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Chicken (3 Pieces) is a popular deep-fried chicken dish consisting of three bone-in or boneless chicken pieces coated in a seasoned wheat-flour batter and submerged in hot oil until golden and crispy. It is a staple of Singapore hawker centres, fast-food outlets, and home cooking, commonly served as a main protein dish at breakfast, lunch, or dinner. The exterior is crunchy and well-seasoned, while the interior remains juicy and tender. Per 100 g, it provides 215 kcal, 19.75 g protein, 12.25 g fat, and 6.42 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 553 mg. Dietary fibre is low at 0.66 g/100 g. The deep-frying process introduces saturated fat (approximately 3.2 g/100 g) and trace trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) from the frying oil, both of which are clinically relevant for cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low) due to the protein-fat matrix blunting the glycaemic response of the wheat-flour batter, yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 7.8 per standard 3-piece serving. Clinically, this food is of high concern for hypertension (high sodium), type 2 diabetes (high fat, low fibre, refined batter), cardiovascular disease (saturated and trans fat, high sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGE generation during frying). It is generally considered halal and is free of dairy but contains gluten from the wheat-flour batter. Consumers are advised to remove the batter and skin where possible, pair with high-fibre vegetables, and limit consumption frequency.
common_user_queries: How many calories in 3 pieces of fried chicken?
Is fried chicken high in sodium?
Is fried chicken suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11020030) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for battered fried chicken products. Saturated fat and trans fat values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for comparable fried chicken entries (USDA SR Legacy item 05062 — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter) to supplement the HPB record, which does not report fatty acid fractions. All other -1 fields reflect genuine absence of data in both HPB and USDA for this specific preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific HPB fried chicken (3 pc) entry is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026-04-16. potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB entry are not reported in the source record; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (item 05062) provides partial micronutrient data for analogous fried chicken but was not used to populate these fields to avoid source mixing without explicit adaptation documentation. trans_fat_g is set to 0.2 g/100 g based on estimation from USDA SR Legacy item 05062 for battered fried chicken; this is an approximation and may vary by oil type and frying conditions. The input record incorrectly tagged 'wholegrain' as a dietary tag — this has been removed as fried chicken with wheat-flour batter is not a wholegrain food. The occasion tag 'breakfast' has been retained in meal_context as fried chicken is consumed at breakfast in Singapore hawker culture. GI value of 0 in the input record has been replaced with an analogous estimate of 45 (low) from the University of Sydney GI Database, as a GI of 0 is not defensible for a food containing 6.42 g carbohydrate per 100 g.
